The Great Pyramid rises in gleaming white limestone, construction ramps spiraling upward as thousands of workers haul blocks toward the clouds.
Gobekli Tepe's T-pillars tower over scattered worshippers, carved stone arms emerging from bedrock beneath an endless Anatolian sky at dawn.
The Ziggurat of Ur climbs skyward in massive mud-brick tiers, its blue-glazed summit shrine dwarfing priests ascending the monumental stairway.
Ramesses II's colossi sit enthroned at Abu Simbel, four painted giants carved from the cliff face, tiny figures gathered between their feet.
Stonehenge's massive trilithons frame the horizon, sarsen megaliths casting long shadows over priests and gathered crowds at midsummer sunrise.
The Ishtar Gate towers in brilliant blue-glazed brick, processional lions and dragons flanking the entrance as merchants pass beneath like ants.
Hatshepsut's pink granite obelisk pierces the sky at Karnak, workers securing its gold-covered pyramidion as the needle catches morning light.
The Great Sphinx stretches across the Giza plateau, painted face gazing eastward, its leonine body swallowing human figures approaching paws.
Carnac's stone rows march across kilometers of Breton coastland, endless menhirs diminishing toward the horizon as druids move between them.
Newgrange's white quartz facade gleams above the Boyne valley, its massive cairn and entrance stone dominating the river plain below.
The Marib Dam spans the desert wadi in colossal stonework, its sluice gates and spillways controlling floodwaters that feed distant green fields.
Etemenanki's seven-tiered mass dominates Babylon, the Tower's summit lost in haze as countless workers swarm its ramps and stairways.
The Step Pyramid at Saqqara rises in six massive limestone tiers, its revolutionary stacked mastabas towering over the desert as priests process below.
Nubian pyramids climb steeply at Meroe, their sharp angles and compact bases crowding the horizon while royal funeral processions wind between them.
Chogha Zanbil's ziggurat ascends in concentric brick squares, its Elamite temple platform dominating the Khuzestan plain beneath merciless Persian sun.
Assyrian lamassu flank Nineveh's gate, winged bull colossi with human heads rising five times human height, their stone bulk shadowing passing soldiers.
The unfinished obelisk lies anchored in Aswan granite, workers swarming its 42-meter length like insects carving stone from living bedrock.
Hattusa's lion gates guard the Hittite capital, massive stone felines carved in relief flanking the entrance beneath towering cyclopean walls.
Malta's Ġgantija temples rise in rough limestone megaliths, their colossal walls and apses dominating the island plateau above the Mediterranean.
Mycenae's Lion Gate stands beneath relieving triangle, massive ashlar blocks and carved felines dwarfing warriors entering the citadel's dark passage.
Babylon's walls stretch endlessly across the plain, their double-rampart brick mass and defensive towers making approaching armies seem like crawling dots.
Sumerian canal headworks divide the Euphrates in massive earthworks, their engineered channels and lock gates reshaping the Mesopotamian floodplain.
Easter Island's ahu platforms bear early moai, volcanic stone ancestors gazing inland, their monolithic forms silhouetted against endless Pacific horizons.
The Walls of Uruk encircle the city in sun-baked brick, their circuit stretching nine kilometers as farmers appear antlike approaching the gates.
The Temple of Karnak's hypostyle hall drowns visitors in stone forest, 134 papyrus columns soaring overhead, their painted capitals vanishing into shadow.
